ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code identified for vegetable cracker; category-level SNOMED mapping not defensible without confirmed code.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of vegetable crackers is approximately 30g, equivalent to about 6–8 thin crackers, commonly consumed as a snack.

nutrient_source.title: Vegetable Cracker — Singapore HPB Foodo Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Vegetable Cracker (F11080075). All core macros and secondary nutrients are taken from this record with confidence 0.95.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI entry for 'vegetable cracker' was found in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 52 is estimated using the closest analogous food: wheat-based crackers (e.g., water crackers, GI ~52–65 range per University of Sydney GI Database). The lower end of 52 is applied given the higher dietary fibre content (5.25g/100g) which moderates glycaemic response. Glycaemic load per 30g serving calculated as: (52 × 38.4g available carbohydrate per 100g × 30g/100g) / 100 = approximately 6.0; using total carbohydrate 64g/100g × 30g/100 = 19.2g per serving, GL = (52 × 19.2)/100 = 9.98 ≈ 10.0.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 421mg per 100g exceeds the 400mg/100g threshold for hypertension-sensitive foods
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit vegetable cracker intake to no more than 20–30g per occasion and monitor cumulative daily sodium from all processed snack sources, as a 30g serving contributes approximately 126mg sodium.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High total carbohydrate (64g/100g) and sugar (16g/100g) with moderate GI (~52) pose glycaemic risk despite 5.25g/100g dietary fibre
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should restrict vegetable cracker portions to approximately 20–30g per serving, pair with a protein or healthy fat source to blunt postprandial glucose response, and account for the 16g/100g sugar content within their daily carbohydrate budget.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium (421mg/100g) and likely saturated fat from vegetable oil blend; high dietary fibre (5.25g/100g) partially offsets cardiovascular risk
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should choose vegetable crackers with verified low saturated fat and no partially hydrogenated oils, limit portions to 30g or less, and consider the sodium contribution within a total daily sodium target of under 2000mg.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Sugar at 16g/100g exceeds the 15g/100g pro-inflammatory threshold; sodium at 421mg/100g also exceeds RA-sensitive limits; processed snack with likely palm-based oils
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should minimise vegetable cracker consumption due to high sugar and sodium content that may promote inflammatory mediators; check product label for palm oil or partially hydrogenated oils.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Vegetable crackers are thin, crispy, baked snack products made primarily from wheat flour combined with various vegetable blends such as spinach, tomato, carrot, or beetroot powders. They are typically ultra-processed and commercially produced, with a light, crunchy texture and a mildly savoury flavour with subtle vegetable notes. Per 100g, they provide 455 kcal, 7.25g protein, 19.25g fat, 64g carbohydrate, 16g sugar, 5.25g dietary fibre, 421mg sodium, 345mg calcium, and 2mg iron. The relatively high dietary fibre content (5.25g/100g) is notable for a cracker product and may contribute to satiety and modest glycaemic moderation. However, the high sodium (421mg/100g) and sugar (16g/100g) content make this product unsuitable for individuals managing hypertension, Type 2 Diabetes, or rheumatoid arthritis without careful portion control. The estimated glycaemic index of approximately 52 (low-medium boundary) reflects the moderating effect of fibre on glucose absorption. Vegetable crackers are commonly consumed as a standalone snack, paired with dips, cheese, or spreads, and are popular in Singapore and across Southeast Asia as a convenient packaged snack. They are suitable for vegetarians and vegans but contain gluten and are not appropriate for those with coeliac disease or wheat allergy.
common_user_queries: How many calories in vegetable crackers?
Are vegetable crackers high in sodium?
What is the GI of vegetable crackers?
Are vegetable crackers suitable for hypertension?
How much fibre do vegetable crackers have?
What are vegetable crackers made of?

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database (F11080075) with confidence 0.95. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database using closest wheat-based cracker equivalent, documented as such. All hard gates pass and all Q criteria are satisfied.
notes: Fields set to -1 (data not available from HPB source): saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g. These fields are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for this product (F11080075) as of the accessed date. Saturated fat breakdown is particularly relevant for cardiovascular risk assessment and should be obtained from product label or manufacturer data when available. GI value of 52 is an analogous estimate based on wheat-based crackers from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI entry for 'vegetable cracker' was found. Halal and kosher status are unconfirmed and set to false pending label verification. The input record listed a GI value of 15 with source 'Embedded GI Database' which is not an approved source under Project Veritas v2; this value has been replaced with the analogous estimate of 52 from the University of Sydney GI Database. The input glycaemic load of 9.6 has been recalculated as 10.0 based on the revised GI and 30g serving size.
